summary: I was born in Greenock, Scotland in 1948, just after the war. Greenock had been heavily bombed during the war because the Germans tried to destroy the shipyards and naval fleets on the River Clyde. Even when I was young, unexploded bombs were still being found, and the town bore the scars of the war. Growing up there, I felt the lingering sadness and economic struggles caused by the war's aftermath.

After the war, Allied policies focused on rebuilding countries like Germany and Japan, which led to modern shipyards being built in those places. This shift caused the decline of the shipbuilding industry on the River Clyde in Scotland, where my father worked. The economic impact was felt deeply in my hometown, as many workers, including veterans, found themselves without jobs. Germany’s rebuilding indirectly shaped my family’s future and the local economy I grew up in.

The River Clyde was central to my early life in Greenock, Scotland, as it was home to the shipyards where my father worked. During the war, the Germans targeted the naval fleets and shipyards on the Clyde, bombing the area heavily but missing their main targets. The shipbuilding industry there declined after the war due to competition from rebuilt shipyards in Germany and Japan. This decline affected my family and community deeply, shaping much of my childhood experience.

After World War II, Japan was rebuilt with modern shipyards as part of Allied policies, which had a big impact on the shipbuilding industry back home in Scotland. This development contributed to the decline of the Clyde shipyards where my father worked. It was ironic and painful to see how the sacrifices made during the war didn’t protect our local industry. Japan’s post-war rise indirectly changed the course of my family’s life.

I was born and raised in Scotland until I was nine, and my early life was deeply rooted in Scottish culture. My family was very connected to our heritage, especially through music and traditions like Scottish country dancing and the Gaelic mod. Scotland’s post-war struggles, especially the decline of shipbuilding, shaped my childhood and family life. Even after moving to North America, my Scottish identity remained a strong part of who I am.

Jimmy Shand was a big part of my childhood soundtrack thanks to my grandfather, who loved Scottish traditional music. We often listened to his accordion music on my grandfather’s old wind-up record player. Though I was shy, I was encouraged to perform Scottish songs at family gatherings, and Jimmy Shand’s music was a joyful connection to my heritage. His tunes helped keep the Scottish spirit alive in our home during those early years.

The Gaelic mod was something my great-grandmother loved deeply; it’s a festival celebrating Scottish Gaelic song, arts, and culture. Growing up, I was aware of this tradition and it helped me feel connected to my Scottish roots. Though I was young, the mod represented the cultural pride my family held onto even as the world around us was changing. It remains an important part of Scotland’s cultural life to this day.

My family moved from Scotland to North America when I was almost nine, in 1957. We first settled in Canada, then later moved to the United States, living in San Diego and finally Seattle. This move uprooted me from the culture I knew, but my Scottish background enriched my experience as I adapted to new places. North America became my home, and I’ve lived in Seattle ever since.

We arrived in Canada in 1957, landing in Quebec City on a ship traveling the St. Lawrence River. My father found work in a shipyard there, and we lived in Quebec for two years. During that time, I learned some French and got to experience Quebec’s unique culture, which was exciting for me as a child. Canada was our first stop in North America and marked the beginning of a new chapter for our family.

Quebec City was where we first arrived in North America in 1957, traveling there by ship along the St. Lawrence River. My father worked in a shipyard there, and we stayed for two years. I learned some French and enjoyed experiencing the local culture, which was quite different from Scotland. Quebec City was my first home in Canada and an important part of my early immigrant experience.

The St. Lawrence River was the waterway we traveled on when we left Scotland and arrived in Quebec City in 1957. It was part of our journey to a new life in Canada. That river marked the beginning of my family’s transition from Scotland to North America. I remember the trip as a significant moment in my childhood.

After living in Canada for two years, my family moved to San Diego, another shipbuilding town where my father found work. This move was part of our ongoing search for stability in North America. San Diego was a stepping stone before we finally settled in Seattle. It was a different environment from Scotland and Canada, but it was part of our journey.

Seattle has been my home since we moved there from San Diego about four years after arriving in North America. It’s where I grew up as a teenager and where I still live today. Seattle shaped much of my adult life, including my education at the University of Washington and my work as a labor organizer. It’s also where I found community in the Seattle Labor Chorus after retirement.

London played an important role in my family history because my maternal grandmother went there to fight for workers’ rights after my grandfather died. She was a widow and lobbied for pensions for women, working with a group of women who eventually won that right. Her leadership in London inspired me and showed me the power of collective action. I’m very proud of her and her legacy.

I attended the University of Washington after high school, which was a transformative experience for me. It opened my eyes to social movements like the anti-Vietnam War protests, women’s rights, and the fight against racism. Coming from Scotland and being new to the U.S., it was exciting but also challenging to engage with these issues. The university was where I began to understand and connect with the broader struggles happening around me.

The Vietnam War was a major issue during my time at the University of Washington in the late 1960s and early 1970s. I was exposed to the anti-war movement there, which was part of a larger awakening to social justice causes. Although I was shy and new to the country, I wanted to be involved in the fight against the war and other injustices. The Vietnam War era shaped much of my early political consciousness.

I eventually worked for SEIU Local 1199NW in the Northwest as an organizer representing healthcare workers. It was a rewarding experience where I got to be involved in labor actions and strikes. We often invited the Seattle Labor Chorus to our events, which brought a special energy and inspiration to our work. My time with SEIU deepened my commitment to economic justice and workers’ rights.

The Seattle Labor Chorus has been a wonderful part of my life, especially after I retired three years ago. I sing tenor in the chorus, which connects me back to my lifelong love of music and my commitment to labor and social justice. The chorus brings people together who share values like economic justice and environmental concerns, creating a warm, family-like community. Their music continues the tradition of singing as part of the labor movement’s struggle for working families.
